title: Leadership style
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Organisational — autocratic, democratic, laissez-faire, and transformational styles.
learningObjectives: Evaluate which style fits different contexts.

simpleExplanation.title: How leaders lead
simpleExplanation.summary: From autocratic command to democratic participation to inspirational transformation — effective style depends on team and task.

flashcards.back: Autocratic (leader decides), democratic (group input), laissez-faire (minimal guidance) — Lewin's child-group studies showed democratic highest satisfaction.
Exchange relationship — rewards for meeting targets, corrective management when standards slip (contingent reward, management by exception).
Idealised influence, inspirational motivation, intellectual stimulation, individualised consideration — raises followers beyond self-interest.
Match style (tell/sell/participate/delegate) to follower readiness (ability + willingness).
Crisis, inexperienced team, time-critical decisions — but risks resentment if overused.
Low productivity and role ambiguity when followers lack expertise or motivation.

sections.content: **Leadership style** (4.2.2) — **Lewin** and **Bass** transformational model are key. Always justify style choice with **task, team maturity, and urgency**.

sections.question: A software start-up's founder makes all product decisions alone (autocratic). After hiring senior developers, she shifts to weekly design workshops (democratic) and eventually lets teams choose their own sprints (delegating). Analyse using Lewin and Hersey-Blanchard.
sections.solution:
**Lewin:** Early **autocratic** style suited a small, inexperienced team needing direction. **Democratic** workshops increased **satisfaction and creativity** as expertise grew — consistent with Lewin's findings that democratic groups show better morale, though not always fastest decisions.

**Hersey-Blanchard:** Junior developers = **low readiness** → **telling/selling**. Senior hires = **high readiness** → **participating/delegating**. Style matched follower **ability and confidence**.

**Bass:** Founder could add **transformational** elements — articulating vision, intellectually stimulating debate — without abandoning structure.

**Evaluation:** **Lewin** used artificial groups; real organisations mix styles. **Situational leadership** is practical but **readiness is hard to measure**. Over-delegation (**laissez-faire**) could cause drift if accountability weakens.
